en.extract:
The black-billed capercaillie (Tetrao urogalloides) is a large grouse species closely related to the more widespread western capercaillie. It is a sedentary species that breeds in the larch taiga forests of eastern Siberia as well as parts of northern Mongolia and China. In the far west of its distribution, the black-billed capercaillie has been known to hybridize with the western capercaillie. Compared to its western cousin, the black-billed capercaillie is also more adaptable to open habitat, given the larch forests it lives in are usually less dense than other taiga communities. Thus, they tend to avoid thick coniferous forests. They are primarily herbivorous, feeding on twigs, buds, leaves, shoots, and berries of various plants. However, their diet in the warmer months includes some insects like caterpillars, larvae, ants and midges.
This species is also known as the eastern capercaillie, Siberian capercaillie, spotted capercaillie or (in Russian) "stone capercaillie".


Description

A male black-billed capercaillie has an average length of about 89–97 cm (35–38 in) and weighs 3.4–4.6 kg (7.5–10.1 lb), with females measuring around 69–75 cm (27–30 in) and weighing 1.7–2.2 kg (3.7–4.9 lb). Their wingspan stretches approximately 75–115 cm (30–45 in) long. Compared to the western capercaillie, it is slightly smaller in size with a slimmer body profile and longer neck, has a black beak (hence its name) instead of being horn-colored, and a longer, more spatulate tail. The eastern capercaillie has a somewhat glossy bluish-black head and neck down to a metallic turquoise breast. Distinct white markings at the tips of its upper and undertail coverts, as well as the wing coverts and flanks, give this grouse the nickname 'spotted capercaillie'. The female is similar to that of its western counterpart, except that its plumage is grayer overall with more heavily scaled underparts, lacks a solid rufous chest unlike the western capercaillie, and has noticeably larger white spots on its wing and tail coverts.


Behavior
In the spring, from dawn until late morning, male capercaillies display by fanning out their tails, puffing their chest feathers out, lowering their wings, and angling their heads upward with an open beak to defend their space from other males and win over the mating rights of females. The size of the males' territories are usually 30 m × 30 m (98 ft × 98 ft), similar to black grouse territories. A group of hens will carefully squat with drooping wings, while the lekking male is present to signal that they are ready for breeding. Their castanets-like call, much louder than the western capercaillie, can be heard up to 1.5 km (0.93 mi) away. It consists of a series of loud repeating clicks ascending in tempo. Other performances used to attract females involve flutter flights. They jump off the ground to heights of 2 m (6 ft 7 in) or to distances of 8–10 m (26–33 ft), rapidly beating their wings during takeoff and landing, generating a thundering flurry that resonates through the dense stands of conifers.


Diet
The black-billed capercaillie is primarily herbivorous. During the winter months, when food is scarce, the black-billed capercaillie primarily feeds on twigs, shoots, and buds from various plant species, as well as the fruit of Rosa acicularis (commonly known as the prickly wild rose). Their main source of twigs and buds is the Siberian larch (Larix dahurica). The male capercaillie seasonally trim young larch trees, stunting their vertical growth. This causes the trees to not only be significantly shorter but also significantly thinner. This creates a unique forest of "dwarf larches," often referred to as a "garden" or "park forest." These gardens are used by the males for foraging. Since the male capercaillie are notably larger, they are unable to perch on branches smaller than 2 cm in diameter, so they tend to forage on the ground. On the other hand, female capercaillie prefer foraging up in the trees. During the winter months, the females stay in higher-altitude, young, denser forests to forage. As the snow begins to melt, the capercaillie shift their diet to include new green shoots and leaves, insects such as caterpillars, larvae, ants, and midges, as well as various berries. This dietary shift, which lasts from spring to autumn, helps female capercaillie gain weight in preparation for reproduction. Flocks will sometimes travel to the edge of the forest to feed on the new sprouting plants.


Status
The black-billed capercaillie is classified as Least Concern on the IUCN Red List due to its wide range across Siberia. However, in China, the species is endangered and classified as a first-class state protection animal (Category I). Over the past century, the black-billed capercaillie population in China has experienced a significant decline. Research conducted by the School of Nature Conservation in Beijing revealed a 35.25% reduction in the species' range in northeast China, since 1970. Several factors have contributed to this decline, including climate change, deforestation, overhunting, and the expansion of urban and agricultural areas. Many of these pressures are a result of China's growing human population, which has led to the expansion of urban areas and farms further north. Additionally, some data has shown that climate change is pushing the species to higher altitudes. In response, China has implemented various measures to protect the black-billed capercaillie and other wildlife. These include efforts such as the Natural Forest Protection Project, the Law of People's Republic of China on the protection of Wildlife project and a policy aimed at restoring farmland to forest. These conservation efforts have proven effective, as recent data indicates that the species' decline is stabilizing.


Taxonomy
The black-billed capercaillie has two recognized subspecies:

T. u. kamtschaticus (Kittlitz, 1858) - the Kamchatka peninsula
T. u. urogalloides (nominate) (Middendorff, 1853) - northeastern Asia

The '''black-billed capercaillie''' ('''''Tetrao urogalloides''''') is a large [[grouse]] species closely related to the more widespread [[western capercaillie]]. It is a sedentary species that [[Breed|breeds]] in the [[larch]] [[taiga]] [[Forest|forests]] of eastern [[Siberia]] as well as parts of northern [[Mongolia]] and [[China]]. In the far west of its distribution, the black-billed capercaillie has been known to [[hybrid (biology)|hybridize]] with the western capercaillie. Compared to its western cousin, the black-billed capercaillie is also more adaptable to open [[habitat]], given the [[larch]] forests it lives in are usually less dense than other taiga communities. Thus, they tend to avoid thick [[Conifer|coniferous]] forests.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Каменный глухарь / Tetrao urogalloides Middendorf, 1851 / Том 2 / Птицы России|url=http://www.egir.ru/bird/45.html|access-date=2021-03-27|website=www.egir.ru}}</ref> They are primarily [[Herbivore|herbivorous]], feeding on twigs, buds, leaves, shoots, and berries of various plants. However, their diet in the warmer months includes some insects like [[caterpillar]]s, [[larva]]e, [[ant]]s and [[midge]]s.<ref name="Andreev-1991" />

This species is also known as the '''eastern capercaillie''', '''Siberian capercaillie''', '''spotted capercaillie''' or (in Russian) "stone capercaillie".<ref name=avibase>{{avibase|117B69091564DBC1|Tetrao parvirostris}}</ref>

==Description==
[[File:Tétras à bec noir.jpg|thumb|Female]]

A male black-billed capercaillie has an average length of about {{cvt|89-97|cm}} and weighs {{cvt|3.4-4.6|kg}}, with females measuring around {{cvt|69-75|cm}} and weighing {{cvt|1.7-2.2|kg}}.<ref>{{Cite journal|last1=de Juana|first1=Eduardo|last2=Kirwan|first2=Guy M.|editor-first1=Josep |editor-first2=Andrew |editor-first3=Jordi |editor-first4=David |editor-first5=Eduardo |editor-last1=Del Hoyo |editor-last2=Elliott |editor-last3=Sargatal |editor-last4=Christie |editor-last5=De Juana |date=2020-03-04|title=Black-billed Capercaillie (Tetrao urogalloides)|url=https://birdsoftheworld.org/bow/species/blbcap1/cur/introduction|journal=Birds of the World|doi=10.2173/bow.blbcap1.01 |s2cid=243304922 |language=en|url-access=subscription }}</ref> Their [[wingspan]] stretches approximately {{cvt|75-115|cm}} long.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Black-billed Capercaillie|url=https://www.oiseaux-birds.com/card-black-billed-capercaillie.html|access-date=2020-07-21|website=www.oiseaux-birds.com}}</ref> Compared to the western capercaillie, it is slightly smaller in size with a slimmer body profile and longer neck, has a black beak (hence its name) instead of being horn-colored, and a longer, more spatulate tail. The eastern capercaillie has a somewhat glossy bluish-black head and neck down to a metallic turquoise breast. Distinct white markings at the tips of its upper and [[Covert feather|undertail coverts]], as well as the [[wing coverts]] and flanks, give this grouse the nickname 'spotted capercaillie'. The female is similar to that of its western counterpart, except that its [[plumage]] is grayer overall with more heavily scaled underparts, lacks a solid [[rufous]] chest unlike the western capercaillie, and has noticeably larger white spots on its wing and [[tail coverts]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=Oriental Bird Club Image Database: Black-billed Capercaillie » Tetrao urogalloides|url=http://orientalbirdimages.org/search.php?Bird_ID=45&Bird_Image_ID=102391|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210205025034/http://orientalbirdimages.org/search.php?Bird_ID=45&Bird_Image_ID=102391|url-status=usurped|archive-date=February 5, 2021|access-date=2020-10-16|website=orientalbirdimages.org}}</ref>

== Behavior ==
In the spring, from dawn until late morning, male capercaillies display by fanning out their tails, puffing their chest feathers out, lowering their wings, and angling their heads upward with an open beak to defend their space from other males and win over the mating rights of females. The size of the males' territories are usually {{cvt|30 x 30|m}}, similar to [[black grouse]] territories. A group of hens will carefully squat with drooping wings, while the [[Lek mating|lekking]] male is present to signal that they are ready for breeding. Their [[castanets]]-like call, much louder than the [[western capercaillie]], can be heard up to {{cvt|1.5|km}} away. It consists of a series of loud repeating clicks ascending in tempo. Other [[Sexual selection in birds|performances]] used to attract females involve flutter flights. They jump off the ground to heights of {{cvt|2|m}} or to distances of {{cvt|8-10|m}}, rapidly beating their wings during takeoff and landing, generating a thundering flurry that resonates through the dense stands of [[conifer]]s.<ref name="Klaus-2012">{{Cite journal |last1=Klaus |first1=Siegfried |last2=Schindlatz |first2=Karl-Heniz |last3=Andreev |first3=Alexander |last4=Bergmann |first4=Hans-Heiner |date=2012-01-01 |title=Ecology and Behaviour of the Black-Billed Capercaillie (''Tetrao urogalloides stegmanni'') in the Khentej Mountains, Mongolia |url=https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/biolmongol/19/ |journal=Erforschung Biologischer Ressourcen der Mongolei |volume=12 |pages=183–191}}</ref><ref>{{Citation|title=Начало.|url=https://ok.ru/video/79935244800|language=en|access-date=2020-10-16}}</ref>

== Diet ==
The black-billed capercaillie is primarily [[herbivorous]]. During the winter months, when food is scarce, the black-billed capercaillie primarily feeds on twigs, shoots, and buds from various plant species, as well as the fruit of ''[[Rosa acicularis]]'' (commonly known as the prickly wild rose).<ref name="Gao-2023"/><ref name="Andreev-1991">{{Cite journal |last=Andreev |first=A. V. |date=1991 |title=Winter Habitat Segregation in the Sexually Dimorphic Black-Billed Capercaillie Tetrao urogalloides |journal=Ornis Scandinavica |volume=22 |issue=3 |pages=287–291 |doi=10.2307/3676606 |jstor=3676606 |issn=0030-5693}}</ref> Their main source of twigs and buds is the Siberian larch (''[[Larix dahurica]]''). The male capercaillie seasonally trim young larch trees, stunting their vertical growth. This causes the trees to not only be significantly shorter but also significantly thinner. This creates a unique forest of "dwarf larches," often referred to as a "garden" or "park forest."<ref name="Andreev-1991" /> These gardens are used by the males for [[foraging]]. Since the male capercaillie are notably larger, they are unable to perch on branches smaller than 2 cm in diameter, so they tend to forage on the ground. On the other hand, female capercaillie prefer foraging up in the trees. During the winter months, the females stay in higher-altitude, young, denser forests to forage.<ref name="Andreev-1991" /> As the snow begins to melt, the capercaillie shift their diet to include new green shoots and leaves, insects such as caterpillars, larvae, ants, and midges, as well as various berries. This dietary shift, which lasts from spring to autumn, helps female capercaillie gain weight in preparation for reproduction.<ref name="Andreev-1991" /> Flocks will sometimes travel to the edge of the forest to feed on the new sprouting plants.<ref name="Klaus-2012"/>

== Status ==
The black-billed capercaillie is classified as [[Least Concern]] on the [[IUCN Red List]] due to its wide range across Siberia.<ref name=iucn/> However, in [[China]], the species is [[Endangered species|endangered]] and classified as a first-class state protection animal (Category I).<ref name="Gao-2023">{{Cite journal |last1=Gao |first1=Xiaodong |last2=Wang |first2=Xibao |last3=Wu |first3=Xiaoyang |last4=Shang |first4=Yongquan |last5=Mei |first5=Xuesong |last6=Zhou |first6=Shengyang |last7=Wei |first7=Qinguo |last8=Sun |first8=Guolei |last9=Dong |first9=Yuehuan |last10=Cui |first10=Weijia |last11=Zhang |first11=Honghai |date=2023-03-03 |title=Comparative Analyses of the Fecal Microbiome of Five Wild Black-Billed Capercaillie (Tetrao parvirostris) Flocks |journal=Animals |volume=13 |issue=5 |page=923 |doi=10.3390/ani13050923 |doi-access=free |issn=2076-2615 |pmid=36899780|pmc=10000248 }}</ref> Over the past century, the black-billed capercaillie population in China has experienced a significant decline. Research conducted by the School of Nature Conservation in [[Beijing]] revealed a 35.25% reduction in the species' range in northeast China, since 1970.<ref name="Yang-2018">{{Cite journal |last1=Yang |first1=Li |last2=Zhang |first2=Chao |last3=Chen |first3=Minhao |last4=Li |first4=Jingxin |last5=Yang |first5=Lei |last6=Huo |first6=Zhaomin |last7=Ahmad |first7=Shahid |last8=Luan |first8=Xiaofeng |date=April 2018 |title=Long-term ecological data for conservation: Range change in the black-billed capercaillie ( Tetrao urogalloides ) in northeast China (1970s–2070s) |journal=Ecology and Evolution |language=en |volume=8 |issue=8 |pages=3862–3870 |doi=10.1002/ece3.3859 |issn=2045-7758 |pmc=5916277 |pmid=29721263|bibcode=2018EcoEv...8.3862Y }}</ref> Several factors have contributed to this decline, including [[climate change]], [[deforestation]], [[Overexploitation|overhunting]], and the expansion of [[Urban area|urban]] and [[Agriculture|agricultural]] areas.<ref name="Yang-2018" /><ref name="Zhang-2020" /> Many of these pressures are a result of China's growing human population, which has led to the expansion of urban areas and farms further north.<ref name="Yang-2018" /> Additionally, some data has shown that climate change is pushing the species to higher altitudes.<ref name="Zhang-2020">{{Cite journal |last1=Zhang |first1=Chao |last2=Xia |first2=Wancai |last3=Luan |first3=Xiaofeng |last4=Zhuang |first4=Hongfei |last5=Khan |first5=Tauheed Ullah |last6=Zhang |first6=Gong |last7=Wu |first7=Shuhong |date=2020-06-01 |title=Use of historical data to assess the impact of climate change and anthropogenic disturbance on the black-billed capercaillie (Tetrao urogalloides) in northeast China |url=https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2351989419307747 |journal=Global Ecology and Conservation |volume=22 |article-number=e00972 |doi=10.1016/j.gecco.2020.e00972 |bibcode=2020GEcoC..2200972Z |issn=2351-9894|doi-access=free }}</ref> In response, China has implemented various measures to protect the black-billed capercaillie and other wildlife. These include efforts such as the Natural Forest Protection Project, the Law of People's Republic of China on the protection of Wildlife project and a policy aimed at restoring [[Agricultural land|farmland]] to forest.<ref name="Zhang-2020" /><ref name="Gao-2023" /> These conservation efforts have proven effective, as recent data indicates that the species' decline is stabilizing.<ref name="Zhang-2020" />

== Taxonomy ==
The black-billed capercaillie has two recognized [[subspecies]]:<ref>{{Cite journal |title=IOC World Bird List 13.1 |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/ioc-lists/crossref |language=en |doi=10.14344/ioc.ml.13.1|doi-access=free |journal=IOC World Bird List}}</ref>

* ''T. u. kamtschaticus'' <small>(Kittlitz, 1858)</small> - the [[Kamchatka Peninsula|Kamchatka peninsula]]
* ''T. u. urogalloides'' (nominate) <small>(Middendorff, 1853)</small> - northeastern [[Asia]]

zh.extract:
黑嘴松鸡（学名：Tetrao urogalloides）为松鸡科松鸡属的鸟类，俗名帮鸡、林鸡。


特征
黑嘴松鸡体重约2752.29克，翼长约341.2毫米，嘴峰长约46.5毫米，喙宽度约15.7毫米，喙厚度约17.4毫米，跗蹠长约60毫米，尾长约261.2毫米。雄鸟体长约0.6米，体羽为黑色，翼羽、覆羽先端和下体杂有白斑。尾呈楔形，尾羽纯黑褐色。雌鸡喉部为乳白色，具黑色细斑。上体呈锈红色，具褐色横斑，羽毛先端为灰色，食性为草食性，主要食物来源是陆生植物。


亚种
T. u. urogalloides：分布于东西伯利亚至北满洲、乌苏里江和库页岛。
T. u. kamtschaticus：分布于堪察加半岛。
T. u. stegmanni：分布于贝加尔湖地区、萨彦山和北蒙古。


分布
分布于俄罗斯、勘察加以及中国大陆的东北等地，多栖息于针叶林、落叶松林、红松林、冷杉林、桦树林以及混交林。黑嘴松鸡在其分布区内为留鸟。该物种的模式产地在斯塔诺夫山。
